John H. Larson, cnai rmA\G
Fai r Po'li ti ca'l Practices
428JStreet,*800
Sacramsnto, CA 95a 14
l. rr o 1r

I 3 rs idt'90
commi aaion
August 2, 1990
Re: Request for Opinion Concerning Potentia'l Conflict
Dear Mr. Larson:
I ao writing this 'letter to obtain guidance on a possibly
sticky issue, I am an attorney and counci'lmember in the city of
A'lameda as wslI as the Republican nomineE for the 13th Assemb'ly
District.
ln January 1990, I became associated with a 'lalr firm, MEYER
& MIToHELL (hereinaftEr M&M), that up unti l November 1989, had
worked on a contract basis for the City of Alameda. (M&M's
representation of the city was terminatgd at thg Novamber 7,
'1989, counci l meeting effective immEdiate'ly although the city
Attorney and councilmemb6r withrow somehow transformsd immediate
tgrmination into meanjng M&M should complete any motions which
were then outstanding, there being one motion pending in fed€ra]
court which I bel'ieve was heard in 6ar]y December 1989, )
I pick my own cases and am not an employee of the firm. I
receive compensation based on the work performed with no specific
requirement that I work a sot number of hours or cases, Indeed,
with all of the positions I hold on both City and County Boards,
my Assembly campaign, and being a wife and mother of two
chi ldren, there are many demands on my time which take a much
higher priority than working as a paid attorney for others.
Prior to my association with M&M, I sought and obtaingd
'legal advice from both my City Attorney, carol Korade, and the
FPPC (oral), that there was no conf 'l'ict. I was simply advised to
abstain on any mstters concerning M&M if they came up. I believe
there was a final payment of fses that was approved on the
consent calendar and from which I abstained prior to going to
work and also prior to discussing any financial terms of
associ ation wi th Mr, Mi tchel l ,
The issue of a potential conflict has arisen in that the
firm repreggnts several police officers in a malicious
prosecution case. M&M had been reta'ined by the city to defend
the city asainst the und6r'lying lawsuit. The police officers
retained M&M prior to M&M not for defense but for affirmative
relief. 
After the city terminated M&M, the police officers
continued M&M's representation as to the maf icious prosecution.
Prior to my association with M&M and 'in the exercise of my
responsibilitigs as a councilmember, I had been contacted by the
poljce officers with their concerns about th6 quality of
reDresentation by the then City Attorney, Carter Stroud' I
reviewed his work on the case, and had some concerns myself.
Thereafter, Mr. Stroud resigned emp'loyment with the city of
Alameda. It has now become appar€nt to me that Mr. stroud's
act'ions may haYe seriously iEopardized the rights of the officers
to recover in the context of a malicious prosscution suit' i'e.
he did not have express authority to settle the case on their


behalf and settled the case in spite of that lack of authonity on
behalf of a'l I parties.
The malicious prosecution suit at this point has received a
defense summary judgment and is on appeal to tho First Appellate
District ciyil No. A049265 by the officers. The factq supporting
my conclusions are ful'ly supported in the Appellants' Opening
Brief. 
M&M is handling the appeal , though I am not invo'lved. If
the officers lose the appeal I am concorned that they wjll have a
right to proceed against the City of Alameda and the City
Attorney, Carter stroud, and must bs so advised. The better
action is for them to be so advised at this point in time so that
they can address the malpractice issue as soon as possible.
while I am not playing any role in this case, I am very
concernod as a counci'lmember that the city Attornsy's office is
continuing with this quality of representation of our employees.
one of the three po'lice officers has brought another malicious
prosecutjon action in another case; again using M&M, Prior to
the officer bringing the malicious prosscution action and in my
role as councilmember, I became aware that the city was being
asked to settte the underlying case. I did not participate in
any council vote concerning the case, and don't be'lisve there was
one. [The majority of the city counci'l has by formal action
given the new City Attorney, Carol Korade, the right to settle
cases up to $25,000 and to hire and fire outside counsel jn what
appears to me to be a direct conf 'lict with the specific
requirements of our charter and clearly an abuse of discretjon by
the Counc i l.l
During the defense of the underlying case, the city
Attorney and outside counsel [BERTRAND, FOX, ELLIOT & COHN],
appeared in federa'l court, the Honorable Judge vukasin presiding,
and sett'led the case on behalf of tho city, again over tho
objections of the officer who was presEnt in Court. They filed
a document which ctearly indicated that they were simply
overruling the officer's obiections and settljng the case; they
made clear that there was a conflict in their rsprosentation of
the officer and the city pointed out to both the court and
oooosinq counse'l that the officer didn't want to settl€ because
he wanted to bring a malicious prosecution suit against the
p'laintiff . 
The officer was not consu'lt6d before the city
Attorney so informed the court. Indeed he had asked the C'ity
Attorney and outside counsel for a copy of the document that they
had fi'led w'ith the court; they both declingd to show it to the
officer or give him a copy. As an attorngy I am aware of guch
things as the attorney cliEnt privi lege, conflicts when
representing multiple parties and the applicable eth'ics statutes
and gu'idel ines. C'lear'ly, Carol Korade did not have written
authorization to rspresent the officer, he had never requested it
under Govt. code 825a nor did she or outside counsel have his
signed consent to settle the case which is required under the
State Bar rules. After disclosing confidential statements to
Caro'l Korade and BERTRAND FOX, ELLIOT & CoHN, they apparent'lv
violated his rishts to confidentjality which I believe applies in
this case even if they did not actually repressnt the officer. '
Again, I am faced with the di'lemma that l am aware of the


quality of 'lega'l representation com'ing out of the City of Alameda
and that the firm 1am associated with, has a responsibi lity to
advise their clients, in this case the police officer, that he
has b6en th6 victim of malpractice by the City Attorney and
outside counse'l she has hired without city council approval.
I have tried my best to balance my responaibi'l ities
without infringing upon the City Attorney's jurisdiction.
However, 1 cannot let th€ qual'ity of representation continue for
the city's employees. Yet there is nothing the majority of the
council will do about it.
I have so notified the council
rgpeatedly. My last resort has been to advise the City Manager
of the quality of representation and that in my opinion, the city
Attorney is generating new causes of action for malpractice
against the city of Alaneda with hor continuing quality of
representation. His rgsponse is that there is nothing that he
can do about it r{ithout, Council action. I advised him that if
he can do nothing to stop it and to see that his employees gEt
compstent representation, that he can anticipate the financjal
impact upon the city's financial rgsourcgs and the 'long term
impact on his employees morale.
(*NOTE: In the last month, the Assistant City Manager sought
lega'l advice from the city Attorney concorning the requirements
of giving notice for a Special Meeting of an advisory board; she
advised him that nong was required. He then participated in a
meeting with the majority of that Board's members and discussed
the subjects which that board regulates, cable TV.
Unfortunately, the city Attorney djd not advise the Assistant
city Manager that the meeting had to open and public and the city
has now had to cure the Brown Act v'iolation as best as possible
retroactive'ly. This 'is not the 'least nor the most significant
shortfal l in legal representation that this city is suffering, it
is gimply one of the most recent. )
I have been in contact with the Grand Jury repeatedly
concerning Brown Act violations that have occurred both upon Ms.
Korade's advice and with her presence. I am not sgeking
confirmation of these violations, I already have that from the
Grand Jury, I am seeking an opinion as to what my role should be
to cure them in the future giYen the constraint that the maiority
of the Counci'l continues to unquestioningly fol'low her advice.
The city Attorney insisted upon the propriety of a closed
session to discuss and agendize the managemqnt-conf i dent i a 1
employee pay resolut'ion (of which she is a memb6r as are the City
Managgr, Assis. city Manager, Psrsonnel Director, al1 of t{hom
were present at the majority of tho meetings') She attended
five of s'ix c'losed msetjngs where fringe benefits and a 5x pay
raise were discussed for the group. I to'ld the City Manager that
I fe'lt very uncomfortable discussing these itoms with the four
members of the Management wage group present' and he to'ld me that
at the next meeting he would ask if anyone wanted him to leave.
He did so, and no one spoke up in the session (I had already
objgcted) so he and everyone e'lss remained. They discussed the
5x pay raise and a dEmand by the Police management for a 9.8x pay
raise. Prior to the nExt meeting, I again presented my concerns
to the C'ity Manager, he countered with his actions at the last


me6tin9. I told him that irrespective of what any other
counci'lmember stated or did not state, I would no longer rema'in
if we went into closed session to discuss these iiems again, if
he and the management group remained. At the next meeting, he
and the City Attorney left, however, the Assis. City Manger and
Personnel director remained. I remained at the moeting but did
not active'ly participate. Nor did the Mayor, who simply asked
for more informaticn concerning salaries,
At the last special mgeting scheduled for consideration of
the pay raise, the Mayor did not take us into closed sossion as
the press was beginning to have a field day with what was
transpiring behind closed doors. The city Attorney indicated
that now w9 could not discuss salaries, fringes, etc, al 1 tho6e
things which we had already discussed and on some reached a
consensus. My concern is what do ws do now to cure the
vjo'lations that have already occurred? l{e are going to meet
within a few days, and I am concerned that what we should do is
start from scratch, I am not sure exactly what that means or how
to jmplement it at this time. I am seeking an opinion as to what
we should do to cure these vio'lations and to avoid their
reoccurrence.
I am also spgcifically seeking an opinion as to how I
should deal w'ith the present situation with the city Attorney.
sh€ has apparently advised the other councilmembers that I could
res'jgn either my position as a councilmember, or terminate my
association with M&M to eliminate any conflicts. Indsed, she has
offerod as evidence to the other councilmembers that upon seeing
a police office outside her door, I told him I hoped he wasn't
being represented by her office, 
My comp'lete statement to him,
which I jokingly offer6d, was that I had hoped he hadn't done
anything for which he needed to be represented by hsr office; 
I
see jt as a very serious situation anytime the City is sugd and
espscially to the police officer when he or she is sued. I
didn't know the officar and had no reason to believe he had done
anything to need lesal representation,
When asked about my apparsnt options as they now stand, the
City Attorney has rofused to render a lega'l opinion to me. Whi'le
I real'ize that she psrsonally has a conflict in so do'ing, she
does not render legal opinions personally, but rather hires
attorneys to provide 'l6gal services for her. I see no reason why
she cannot hire independent counsel to advise me as to whether or
not I have a confl ict in performing my official responsibi l ities
and how to best alleviate any such conf 'licts, espgcially since
she has hired outs'ide counsel to perform the inquiry for
Counci lmember Withrow.
I am forwarding you a few pertinent pieces of documentation
so that you may get a ful'l grasp of the situation. If I may be
of any assistance please don't hesitat€ to call.
Yours very tre1y, /2
2.",4,."--1n . -ru?,,---'
Barbara M. Thomas

sections.question_synthetic: An attorney serving as both a city councilmember and Assembly candidate seeks FPPC guidance on potential conflicts of interest arising from her association with a law firm that previously represented the city and now represents police officers in litigation against the city, as well as guidance on her role in addressing Brown Act violations and conflicts involving the City Attorney.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No formal question or conclusion sections are present. The document is heavily narrative in nature with multiple complex fact patterns and issues interwoven. The letter appears incomplete (truncated). High complexity and length make full extraction challenging. No clear delineation of specific legal questions asked.

embedding.summary: A city councilmember and attorney requests FPPC guidance on multiple conflict of interest issues including her association with a law firm representing police officers in litigation against the city, her concerns about the City Attorney's legal representation quality and alleged Brown Act violations, and her role in addressing these governance issues.
